{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient burial mounds known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient burial mounds known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient burial mounds known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.

* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)] <!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine-->

<!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine--><!--Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine-->

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High Rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]
{{translated page|ja|新屋坐天照御魂神社|version=99452665|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High Rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]
{{translated page|ja|新屋坐天照御魂神社|version=99452665|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = [[File:Niiya Jinja Nishihukui Honden.jpg|250px|main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui]] | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[:en:Ibaraki, Osaka|Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|50|48.60|N|135|32|57.81|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Amateru Mitama no Mikoto (Heavenly Illuminating Spirit) | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Ibaraki|Ibaraki]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High Rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

revision.text:
{{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11501968]] Wikidata name: Niiya-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = File:新屋神社（福井）1.jpg | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[:en:Ibaraki, Osaka|Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.846833|N|135.549392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602}} | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 | prefecture= {{ill|Ibaraki|qid=Q502403}} | architecture_style= {{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}} }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Ibaraki|Ibaraki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High Rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]
{{translated page|ja|新屋坐天照御魂神社|version=99452665|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
{{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11501968]] Wikidata name: Niiya-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = File:新屋神社（福井）1.jpg | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[:en:Ibaraki, Osaka|Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.846833|N|135.549392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602}} | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 | prefecture= {{ill|Ibaraki|qid=Q502403}} | architecture_style= {{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}} }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Ibaraki|Ibaraki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High Rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]
{{translated page|ja|新屋坐天照御魂神社|version=99452665|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Niiya-jinja]][[ja:新屋坐天照御魂神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11501968]] Wikidata name: Niiya-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = File:新屋神社（福井）1.jpg | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[:en:Ibaraki, Osaka|Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.846833|N|135.549392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602}} | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 | prefecture= {{ill|Ibaraki|qid=Q502403}} | architecture_style= {{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}} }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Ibaraki|Ibaraki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High Rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]
{{translated page|ja|新屋坐天照御魂神社|version=99452665|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Niiya-jinja]][[ja:新屋坐天照御魂神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11501968]] Wikidata name: Niiya-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = File:新屋神社（福井）1.jpg | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[:en:Ibaraki, Osaka|Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.846833|N|135.549392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}} | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 | prefecture= {{ill|Ibaraki|WD=Q502403}} | architecture_style= {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Ibaraki|Ibaraki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High Rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]
{{translated page|ja|新屋坐天照御魂神社|version=99452665|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Niiya-jinja]][[ja:新屋坐天照御魂神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11501968]] Wikidata name: Niiya-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = File:新屋神社（福井）1.jpg | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[:en:Ibaraki, Osaka|Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.846833|N|135.549392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}} | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 | prefecture= {{ill|Ibaraki|WD=Q502403}} | architecture_style= {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Ibaraki|Ibaraki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High Rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Niiya-jinja]][[ja:新屋坐天照御魂神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11501968]] Wikidata name: Niiya-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine | image = File:新屋神社（福井）1.jpg | caption = main hall of the shrine in Nishifukui | location = [[:en:Ibaraki, Osaka|Ibaraki, Osaka]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.846833|N|135.549392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}} | founded = (Traditional) During the reign of Emperor Sujin (10th Emperor) | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | festival = October 16 | prefecture= {{ill|Ibaraki|WD=Q502403}} | architecture_style= {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
The '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine''' (新屋坐天照御魂神社, Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Jinja) refers to three Shinto shrines located in [[:en:Ibaraki|Ibaraki]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. These shrines are:
* '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishifukui)''': The central shrine, considered the main site. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Shukunosho)'''. * '''Niiya ni Masu Amateru Mitama Shrine (Nishikawara)'''.
These shrines are believed to be interconnected, either as branches or representing the three "seats" mentioned in ancient records. They are enshrined in the "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" (an ancient record of official shrines) as a group of three significant deities.
== Deities == The shrines primarily enshrine '''Amateru Mitama no Mikoto''', regarded as a heavenly illuminating deity. Additional associated deities include:
* '''Hikohono Ninigi no Mikoto''': A deity connected to the founding myth of Japan. * '''Hikohono Akari no Mikoto''': Another deity linked with the light of the heavens.
== Historical Significance == The shrines date back to the early Heian period and are mentioned in historical documents like the '''Engishiki'''. They were significant sites during their time, recognized as important shrines under the "Meishin Taisha" designation (Shrines of High Rank).
=== Nishifukui Shrine === Located in Nishifukui, this shrine is considered the main site. It features historical architecture, including a '''nagare-zukuri''' (flowing style) main hall and connections to ancient Kofuns known as the Niiya Tomb Cluster.
=== Shukunosho Shrine === Traditionally said to have been established during the reign of Empress Jingū, this shrine is associated with the division of worship from the main shrine. It serves as a significant local shrine for the area.
=== Nishikawara Shrine === This shrine was originally located at another site and moved to its current location in the Edo period. It is connected to local agricultural rituals and serves as a guardian of the region.
== festivals and Practices == The shrines hold festivals, with the main celebration on '''October 16'''. These include rituals honoring the deities and local community activities.
== Modern Status == All three shrines are considered historical landmarks and continue to serve as places of worship. The Nishifukui Shrine operates as an independent shrine not affiliated with the broader Shinto Shrine Association.
== Access == * Nishifukui Shrine: Access via Hankyu Bus from JR Ibaraki Station. * Shukunosho Shrine: Close to Toyokawa Station on the Osaka Monorail. * Nishikawara Shrine: Approximately 1.1 km from Sojiji Station on the Hankyu Line.
== External Links == * [http://niiyajinja.jp Official Website of Nishifukui Shrine] * [https://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/ Niiya Shrines Overview (Kokugakuin University)]
